coredata.dc:title: One-step microwave synthesis of high-efficiency solid-state luminescent carbon dots with aggregation-induced emission
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jiusheng
Tan, Wenjiang
Wang, ChenXu
Yan, LiHe
Si, Jinhai
Hou, Xun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are biophilic quantum dots with excellent luminescent properties, however, the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect is a serious limitation for their application in solid-state devices. In this manuscript, a solid-state luminescent blue CDs (B-CDs) with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 52% was prepared by a microwave-assisted method. The B-CDs showed weak fluorescence and with a photoluminescence fluorescence quantum yield (PLQY) of only 0.63% in aqueous solution, but exhibited an aggregation-induced emission in the solid state. The AIE phenomenon of B-CDs was shown to result from the inhibition of surface chromophore motion during aggregation by characterizing the PL intensity under different ratios of DCM (poor solvent). Furthermore, the fluorescence kinetic mechanism associated with the B-CDs was investigated by the time-resolved PL. Finally, a blue LED with CIE coordinates of (0.15, 18) was fabricated using B-CDs as the phosphor, showing a promising potential application in solid-state optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent
Aggregation-induced emission
Solid state fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of photophysical properties and AIE behaviors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis of B-CDs; (b) STEM image of the B-CDs (Inset: HR-TEM image of B-CDs); (c) Particle size distribution measured by TEM; (d) Raman spectrum of the B-CDs; (e) FT-IR spectrum of B-CDs.
(a) XPS spectra of B-CDs. High resolution XPS spectra and the corresponding fitting curves of (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s and (d) S 2p of B-CDs.

(a) UV/Vis absorption (black line), PL excitation (red line) and PL emission (blue line) of B-CD (insets: Photographs under daylight and UV light of B-CDs powder). PL spectra of B-CD in the (b) aggregated state and (c) dispersed state under different excitations. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
TEM images of B-CDs dispersed in bad solvent percentage (a) 0% and (b) 90% solutions.

PL spectra of the as-prepared B-CDs solution with varying fractions of (a) DCM/DMF (fb: fraction of bad solvents) and (c) glycerine/water (from 0% to 90%, Ex = 380 nm). Trend of B-CDs's PL intensity at ∼450 nm, varying with the ratio of (b) DCM/DMF. (d) PL spectra of the B-CDs powder dissolved in aqueous solvents at various concentrations from 0.1 to 10 mg mL-1. (e) PL spectra of the B-CDs powder dissolved in aqueous solvents at various temperatures from 273 K to 373 K. (f) Fluorescence decay curve of B-CDs solution (DCM/DMF = 0% (red line), 90% (blue line)) and powder (black line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic and energy level diagram of the effect of AIE for B-CDs.
(a) Emission spectra of the LED devices fabricated by using B-CDs, and its (b) CIE color coordinate.

QY,
τ
,
K r
and
K
n r
of B-CD solution and powder.

head.ce:title: One-step microwave synthesis of high-efficiency solid-state luminescent carbon dots with aggregation-induced emission

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Physical Electronics and Devices of the Ministry of Education and Shaanxi Key Lab of Information Photonic Technique, School of Electronics and Information Engineering, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Physical Electronics and Devices of the Ministry of Education and Shaanxi Key Lab of Information Photonic Technique
School of Electronics and Information Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Physical Electronics and Devices of the Ministry of Education and Shaanxi Key Lab of Information Photonic Technique, School of Electronics and Information Engineering, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, 710049, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are biophilic quantum dots with excellent luminescent properties, however, the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect is a serious limitation for their application in solid-state devices. In this manuscript, a solid-state luminescent blue CDs (B-CDs) with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 52% was prepared by a microwave-assisted method. The B-CDs showed weak fluorescence and with a photoluminescence fluorescence quantum yield (PLQY) of only 0.63% in aqueous solution, but exhibited an aggregation-induced emission in the solid state. The AIE phenomenon of B-CDs was shown to result from the inhibition of surface chromophore motion during aggregation by characterizing the PL intensity under different ratios of DCM (poor solvent). Furthermore, the fluorescence kinetic mechanism associated with the B-CDs was investigated by the time-resolved PL. Finally, a blue LED with CIE coordinates of (0.15, 18) was fabricated using B-CDs as the phosphor, showing a promising potential application in solid-state optoelectronic devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of light-emitting, nanomaterial whose size is usually less than 10 nm [1]. CDs have exhibited many excellent properties since its inception, such as biocompatibility, high luminescence stability, low cost, and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) [2–4], making it potentially valuable for applications in bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, photocatalysis, sensing, etc. [5–7].

A light-emitting diode (LED) is a kind of solid-state optoelectronic device that can directly convert electrical energy into light and has the simultaneous advantages of small size, high luminescence performance, long lifetime, and low power consumption [8]. To date, rare earth-based phosphors and quantum dots have been reported as light conversion materials for devices, but these materials are generally expensive and toxic, which limits their application in LEDs [9–11]. CDs have great potential for applications in LEDs because of their low cost, environmental friendliness, and wide range of color tunability [12].

However, most of the CDs reported thus far are of the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) type, which means that the fluorescence of CDs is severely weakened or even quenched at high concentrations or in the solid-state [13–16]. One of the possible quenching mechanisms is that the chromophores inside CDs are mostly planar aromatic ring structures, and in the aggregated state, the strong π-π stacking interactions between the carbon cores in contact with each other cause the energy level to split into a nearly continuous energy band, so the energy can be easily consumed by nonradiative motion, which eventually leads to the sudden quenching of fluorescence [17,18]; this greatly limits the application of CDs in solid-state optoelectronic devices, so it is particularly important to develop CDs with solid-state fluorescence. Most of the current reports on anti-aggregation quenching carbon dots use the doping of CDs into solid matrices or the introduction of polymer chains into CDs [19,20]. For example, Qu et al. prepared carbon dots that glow green in solution by microwave-assisted method using citric acid (CA) and urea. CDs@starch composite phosphors were obtained by forming hydrogen bonds that allowed starch granules to adsorb CDs, achieving CDs luminescence in the solid-state [21]. However, these methods have a common drawback, which is the fluorescence will still be quenched when doped with higher concentrations of CDs.

Since the discovery of the AIE phenomenon by B. Z Tang and his colleagues in 2001 [22], AIE materials have found important applications in OLED displays, illumination materials, sensing materials, bioimaging, and other fields [23–26]. This group found that fluorescent materials with AIE effects could be obtained by cleverly designing the molecular structure to effectively overcome the ACQ phenomenon. Therefore, the introduction of the AIE effect into CDs will be an effective solution to realize the luminescence of solid-state CDs, and it is foreseeable that the luminescence of this CDs in the aggregated state will not be limited by its concentration. Surprisingly, an increasing number of researchers have started to focus on the combination of AIE theory and carbon quantum dots. H Yang et al. [27] made the first attempt to introduce AIE theory and synthesized CDs with a symmetric heterocyclic structure around a disulfide bond in the surface state, which shows blue emission in the dispersed state and red emission in the aggregated state due to the restriction of intramolecular rotation in the surface state. XK Xu et al. [28], on the basis of H Yang, further found that the spectrum of solid carbon dots could be adjusted from green to red by increasing the nitrogen content in the precursor. However, all the above AIE CDs are produced by the dangerous hydrothermal method and are not suitable for large-scale production. It is beneficial to develop safer and more environmentally friendly methods of producing AIE CDs.

Here, we report a facile method for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (B-CDs) with efficient solid-state blue light by microwave-assisted heating. In particular, we found that B-CDs possesses classical aggregation-induced luminescence properties-B-CDs shows weak blue light emission (PLQY = 0.63%) in a good solvent (water), whereas their PLQY is significantly enhanced to 52.23% in solid-state aggregates. The large difference in PLQY between B-CD in liquid and solid state aroused our research interest. First, we confirmed that the fluorescence behaviour of B-CD in the solid state was dominated by the surface chromophores of carbon dots by varying the excitation wavelength PL. Then we found that the AIE phenomenon of B-CD may be related to the restricted intramolecular motion of the chromophores on the surface of the carbon dots after aggregation through the tests of variable bad solvent percentage PL, variable solvent viscosity PL, variable temperature PL, and variable concentration PL. Furthermore, through the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of B-CDs, we depicted the mechanism of fluorescence dynamics before and after B-CDs aggregation. Finally, based on the excellent solid-state luminescence performance of B-CDs, we combined it with UV chips to prepare blue LEDs to demonstrate the potential application of AIE-CDs in solid-state optoelectronic devices.

Phloroglucinol (PG) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. l-cysteine (L-cys) was obtained from Energy Chemical Co., Ltd. Boric acid (BA), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), Dichloromethane (DCM), and Ethanol (EtOH) were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All the chemicals were used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used in the whole research.

PG, L-cys and BA in a molar ratio of 1:1:3 were added to 10 ml of deionized water and then sonicated for 10 min to treat to a clear solution. The solution was transferred to a microwave reactor and heated at 400w for 5 min with stirring during heating to prevent flash boiling and to homogenize the reaction. 20 mL of ethanol was added to the reacted beaker and the precipitate was washed three times by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 5 min. The precipitate was then dissolved in water and a needle filter of 0.22 μm was used to remove large particles, and finally the resulting solution was freeze-dried into a white powder as B-CDs.
L-CDs were prepared by the same process as B-CDs, with the difference that no boric acid was added.

According to the method commonly used in the field of AIE [29] when preparing Nano-aggregates — dispersing the sample solution in a series of mixed solutions of bad solvents/good solvents. Specifically in this paper, solutions of equal amounts of B-CDs were configured into a series of mixed solutions with DCM/DMF of 0%–90%. As can be seen from Supporting Information Fig. S1, B-CDs powder can be well dispersed in DMF solution, and is almost insoluble in DCM solution, and the DCM and the DMF are mutually soluble, so as the proportion of DCM in the mixed solution climbs, B-CDs will inevitably become more and more concentrated in the DMF portion in the mixed solution, which is equivalent to disguise to increase the concentration of B-CDs. Thus, when DCM/DMF = 90%, we can observe the formation of B-CDs aggregates from both macroscopic (Supporting Information Fig. S1) and microscopic (Fig. 4) levels.

InGaN LED chips were purchased from Shenzhen XuSheng Electronic Technology Co., Ltd. The chips emitting 365 nm light with a working voltage of 3.0 V and a maximum driving power of 1W were the excitation light source of the LED lamp. B-CDs were mixed well with the UV curing adhesive and drop coated on the chip, then irradiated with 365 nm UV lamp for 5min to fully cure, and finally the bead cover is put on to get the LED.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken with a Talos F200X instrument operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The Raman spectra were obtained with a Renishaw inVia Qontor instrument. FT-IR measurements were taken using a Bruker VERTEX70 instrument. The XPS spectra were obtained using a ESCALAB Xi + X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The steady-state absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of samples were obtained with a UV-2600 and FLS920 (Edinburgh) spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) were measured by an FLS980 (Edinburgh) instrument coupled with an integrating sphere lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), and the excitation wavelength is 410 nm. The samples were dispersed in DMF/DCM mixed solution and measured in a quartz cell with a path length of 10 mm at room temperature.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, PG, L-cys and BA were used as precursors in this paper to prepare B-CDs in one step by a microwave-assisted method. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 1b and c) show that B-CDs consists of monodisperse nanoparticles with an average particle size of 6.81 nm. In addition high resolution TEM (HR -TEM) (inset of Fig. 1b) shows that B-CDs has lattice stripes with a crystalline spacing of 0.32 nm, which corresponds to the (002) crystalline surface of graphite [15]. In addition, the Raman spectrum of B-CDs was also characterized, as shown in Fig. 1d, which shows two distinct peaks at 1363 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1, corresponding to the D-band and G-band, respectively. In general, the D-band represents the vibration of disordered or defective carbon, while the G-band represents the vibration of well-organized graphitic carbon [30]. The value of IG/ID as the ratio of sp2/sp3 carbon is often used to show the degree of graphitization of CDs; the larger the ratio is, the higher the degree of graphitization of CDs [30,31]. The convolution of the Raman spectra by Lorentz fitting gives an IG/ID of 0.43, indicating the disorder of the B-CDs surface.

Then Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization of B-CDs were performed to study the chemical composition, structure and indicated functional groups of B-CDs. As shown in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1e), the broader absorption band at 3198 cm−1 is believed to be from B-CDs indicating O–H and N–H stretching vibrations [32], and strong peaks at 1610 cm−1 and 1004 cm−1 reflect the presence of C=O and C–O–C bonds, respectively, suggesting that L-cys with PG and boronic acid are associated with each other through the occurrence of an esterification reaction [15]. The characteristic stretching vibration of C=C was observed at 1400-1600 cm−1 and the absorption band at 1346 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of B–O [33]. The absorption bands at 2580 cm−1 and 491 cm−1 correspond to S–H and -S-S- stretching vibrations, respectively, which may indicate the formation of the disulfide bond structure of L-cys during polymerization [34]. These results are consistent with the XPS analysis. In Fig. 2 a, the XPS spectrum confirms that B-CDs consists of C, O, N, S, and B, which indicates that B and S have been successfully doped into CD. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 2b) reveals the presence of C=O, C=C, C–O, and C–N. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2c) is then deconvoluted into two contributions, C=O and C–O. The S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2d), on the other hand, shows the presence of C–S and -S-S-, which further confirms the existence of a disulfide bond structure. Besides, the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S5) show that the B-CDs have a bun diffraction peak near 24° and 41°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of graphite, respectively [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of photophysical properties and AIE behaviors
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 3
we characterized the basic spectral information of the B-CDs solution: UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence excitation (PLE), and photoluminescence (PL). The UV–vis absorption spectra show that B-CDs has an absorption peak at ∼250 nm, which is usually attributed to the π-π* transitions from C=C in the core of B-CD [35,36], while the side peaks at 300–400 can be attributed to the n-π* transitions from the C=N/C=O in the carbon dot surface state [35]. The PLE spectra of B-CDs show that their optimal excitation wavelength is ∼390 nm, while the main peak of the PL spectra is located at ∼450 nm, and the full width at half maximum is ∼64 nm. The inset picture shows that the B-CDs powder emits bright blue fluorescence under excitation with a UV lamp at 365 nm, and its absolute photoluminescent quantum yield (PLQY) is 52.28%, while the B-CDs solutions show a rather low PLQY level of 0.63% (Fig. S4). To investigate the reason for the large PLQY difference between B-CDs powder and solution, the following study was carried out.

According to the method commonly used in the field of AIE [29], in preparing Nano-aggregates, the sample solution is used in a series of mixed solutions of bad solvents/good solvents. As shown in Fig. S1a, DMF and DCM were chosen as good and bad solvents, respectively, because B-CDs has good solubility in DMF and very poor solubility in DCM, and DMF and DCM are mutually soluble. Therefore, as the proportion of DCM in the mixed solution increases, B-CDs will inevitably become more and more concentrated in the DMF portion in the mixed solution, which is equivalent to masking to increase the concentration of B-CDs. Furthermore, solutions of equal amounts of B-CDs were configured into a series of mixed solutions with DCM/DMF of 0%–90%. Thus, when DCM/DMF = 90%, the Nano-aggregates of B-CDs was observed from both macroscopic (Fig. S1) and microscopic (Fig. 4) levels. In the following section, we call it the dispersed state of B-CDs when the fraction of DCM is 0%, and correspondingly when the fraction of DCM is 90%, we call it the aggregated state of B-CDs.

As shown in Fig. 3b, with the gradual increase in the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 420 nm, the fluorescence emission intensity of B-CDs in the aggregated state increases and then decreases, but the main peak of fluorescence emission is always maintained at approximately 460 nm, while Fig. 3c shows that the main peak of fluorescence emission of B-CDs in the dispersed state gradually shifts from ∼420 nm to ∼450 nm, showing the typical excitation wavelength-dependent characteristics of carbon dots. According to a related study [16,37], the intrinsic emission from the carbon core usually has excitation wavelength-dependent properties, while the fluorescence emission from the surface state usually has no excitation wavelength-dependent properties. Based on the above analysis, we believe that the fluorescence signal of B-CDs in the aggregated state at ∼460 nm should come from the surface state.

Then, we investigated the PL spectra of B-CDs in DCM/DMF solutions with different ratios. As shown in Fig. 5 a, we can observe the classical AIE phenomenon - the PL intensity of B-CDs is enhanced with the increase of fb. When fb is equal to 90%, the PL intensity of B-CDs is enhanced to 16 times the initial intensity, as shown in Fig. 5b. For this phenomenon, based on the restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM) theory in the field of AIE [38], we proposed a hypothesis that a surface state with a special structure is formed on the surface of B-CDs, and the weak fluorescence displayed by B-CDs in the dispersed state can be attributed to the intramolecular nonradiative motion of this surface state, while in the aggregated state, this intramolecular nonradiative motion is effectively restricted, resulting in intense bright blue fluorescence emission.

A related study [39] found that the RIM process of AIE groups can also occur in highly viscous solvents, and based on this strategy, we investigated the PL spectra of B-CDs in solutions of different viscosities. We dispersed the same amount of B-CDs as-prepared solution in a series of mixed solutions with different water-glycerol ratios to achieve different viscosity solution environments. Predictably, the viscosity of the mixed solutions increased with increasing the glycerol ratio. As shown in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs increased with increasing glycerol ratio, and its fluorescence intensity was ∼32 times higher than that at the initial time when the glycerol ratio reached 90%. This result strongly indicates that limiting the intramolecular nonradiative motion of the B-CDs surface state can effectively improve the PL intensity of B-CDs; furthermore, we supplemented the concentration experiment and the variable temperature experiment, as shown in Fig. 5d, and the fluorescence intensity of the B-CDs solution increased continuously with its concentration. As shown in Fig. 5e, the fluorescence intensity of the B-CDs solution continuously decreases with increasing temperature, which can be attributed to the fact that elevated temperature intensifies the intramolecular nonradiative motion of the surface state. The above analysis strongly supports our theoretical explanation of the B-CDs AIE phenomenon.

Considering that the molar ratio of precursors may affect the PLQY of B-CDs, we synthesized four other molar ratios of B-CDs, as shown in Fig. S2, and the results showed that the optimal molar ratio of PG: L-cys: BA was 1:1:3 (Tables S1 and S2). Further by controlling the different heating time and heating power for synthesizing B-CDs, we determined the optimal heating time to be 4 min and the optimal heating power to be 500 w (Fig. S3). Furthermore, the role of B-dopant in the entire system has been investigated: (1) Comparative studies were conducted using B-less CDs (L-CDs), which still exhibit solid-state luminescence but with significantly lower PL intensity compared to B-CDs (Fig. S7 a and b). This is due to the fact that B doping facilitates the suppression of excitation of the relatively short edge/surface states of carrier complexes in CDs [40]; (2) Additionally, L-CDs also demonstrate AIE properties (Fig. S7c), where PL gradually increases with an increasing fraction of bad solvent. In contrast, when subjected to AIE testing, the PL of B-CDs exhibits a sudden enhancement when the fraction of bad solvent exceeds 80% (Fig. 5a). The presence of B atoms leads to the formation of B–OH hydrogen bonds during CDs aggregation [41], further promoting cross-linking and immobilization of surface states on CDs [42]. Therefore, it is reasonable to speculate that the doped B atoms form B–OH hydrogen bonds after aggregation, effectively inhibiting non-radiative motions associated with surface states on CDs.

The absolute PLQY of B-CDs in the solid state was as high as 52.23% (Fig. S4a), while the absolute PLQY of B-CDs measured in DMF solution (4 mg ml−1) was only 0.63% (Fig. S4b), which is much lower than its performance in the solid state, exhibiting the typical aggregation-induced enhanced emission phenomenon. The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of B-CDs in solution (DMF/DCM = 0%, 90%) and powder when excited at 380 nm are shown in Fig. 5f. First, we note that the fluorescence lifetime curves of B-CDs in solution (90%) are similar to those in powder, which indicates the formation of aggregates similar to those in the solid state at this point in solution. The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of B-CDs in solution (0%) and powder were fitted by using second-order exponential functions (Table S3) with average fluorescence lifetimes of 3.83 ns and 5.28 ns, respectively, and then the corresponding
K r
(radiation rate constant) and
K
n r
(non-radiation rate constant) were calculated according to the following equations:
(1)
K r
=
Q Y
τ
(2)
K
n r
=
1 − Q Y
τ

The calculated results are shown in Table 1 , where the
K r
of B-CDs increases from
1.64 ×
10
− 3
to
9.89 ×
10
− 2
from the dispersed to the aggregated state, while the
K
n r
decreases dramatically from
2.59 ×
10
− 1
to
9.05 ×
10
− 2
. It is therefore reasonable to speculate that the reason for the large difference in the PLQY of B-CDs in powder and solution is that aggregation may lead to an inhibition of surface group motion and a significant decrease in the nonradiative rate. Furthermore, we observed that the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs both in solution and in powder can be resolved into two fluorescent species - fast PL
τ 1
and slow PL
τ 2
, and from the dispersed to the aggregated state, the percentage of fast PL
τ 1
decreases from 50.07% to 9.24%, while the percentage of slow PL
τ 2
increases from 49.93% to 90.76% (Table S3). We speculate that there may be two radiation paths for B-CDs: (1) fast PL
τ 1
: direct radiation complex of carriers on the carbon core and (2) slow PL
τ 2
: relaxation of carriers on the carbon nucleus to the surface state followed by radiative recombination. Therefore, after the above experiments, we drew a schematic diagram of the AIE mechanism of B-CDs as shown in Fig. 6 .

Due to its excellent performance in solid state luminescence, B-CDs has been used as a phosphor for the preparation of blue LEDs. We thoroughly mixed B-CDs with epoxy resin, and then dropped the mixed material onto an LED chip (365 nm EX) for encapsulation. As shown in Fig. 7 , the spectral emission of the blue LED prepared using B-CDs is located at ∼460 nm with International Commission on Illumination (CIE) coordinates of (0.15,0.18), which shows that AIE-CDs have a good potential for application in solid-state optoelectronic devices. The stability of the prepared B-CDs was assessed by subjecting them to a 365 nm UV lamp for 210 min. As shown in Fig. S8a
, the B-CDs exhibited excellent photostability. Furthermore, the sustained operational performance of the corresponding LEDs also confirmed the remarkable stability of the B-CDs (Fig. S8b).

In summary, a B-CDs with efficient solid-state blue light emission was prepared through microwave-assisted strategy in this work. Compared with the weak fluorescence in water (good solvent) (PLQY = 0.63%), the as-synthesized B-CDs showed a higher PLQY of 52.23% in solid state, which could be attributed to an AIE effect. Then, AIE effect of the B-CDs was confirmed by characterizing the PL intensity under different ratios of DCM (bad solvent). We further demonstrated the AIE effect by increasing the viscosity (dispersed the B-CDs in glycerine/water), concentration and temperature of the B-CDs aqueous solution. Furthermore, a fluorescence kinetic mechanism associated with B-CDs’ AIE was proposed based on time-resolved PL: the rate of radiation recombination and lifetime of carrier were improved under solid state when compared with aqueous solution. Finally, a blue LED with CIE coordinates of (0.15, 18) was fabricated with B-CDs as the phosphor and show a promising potential application in solid-state optoelectronic devices.

Jiusheng Hu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wenjiang Tan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. ChenXu Wang: Conceptualization. LiHe Yan: Supervision. Jinhai Si: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xun Hou: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science of China (62027822, 62175197); Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi Province of China (2018JM6012). the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019YFA0706402).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2024.115219.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lim, S. Y., Shen, W. & Gao, Z. Q. Carbon quantum dots and their applications. Chemical Society Reviews 44, 362-381, doi:10.1039/c4cs00269e (2015).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Doped carbon dots enable highly efficient multiple-color room temperature phosphorescence

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient and high color rendering index multilayer luminescent solar concentrators based on colloidal carbon quantum dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Drug-derived bright and color-tunable N-doped carbon dots for cell imaging and sensitive detection of Fe3+ in living cells
